coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots derived from urine and their application for bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xue-Di
Li, Jun
Niu, Jia-Ning
Bao, Xuan-Ping
Zhao, Hai-Dong
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: This study aims to obtain water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from low-value metabolites through a simple, economical, one-step synthetic route. The urine C-dots (UCDs) and hydrothermally treated urine C-dots (HUCDs) were obtained, respectively, using straightforward Sephadex filtration method from human adults and hydrothermal reaction method. The UCDs and HUCDs emit fluorescence upon being excited with ultraviolet light with a quantum yield of 4.8% and 17.8%, respectively. TEM analysis revealed that UCDs and HUCDs had an average size of 2.5 nm and 5.5 nm, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis showed the UCDs and HUCDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy demonstrated the presence of functional groups, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxylate and carbonyl groups onto the C-dots. The UCDs and HUCDs can be directly used for in vivo and in vitro imaging in Hela cells, Caenorhabditis elegans, onion epidermal cells and bean sprouts. The cytotoxicity study revealed that the UCDs and HUCDs were not toxic to normal rat kidney (NKR) cells with good biocompatibility. The results revealed that the C-dots derived from urine have good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence and may have potential to be a safe fluorescent probe for bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Urine
Fluorescence
Bio-imaging
Cytotoxicity

Concentration effect on fluorescence intensity of the C-dots
Characterization of the UCDs and HUCDs
XPS analysis of the UCDs and HUCDs
Fluorescent properties of the UCDs and HUCDs
Cytotoxicity to normal cells of urine C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of UCDs and HUCDs. Inset: photographs of UCDs (1) and HUCDs (1′) under white light and UV light; Fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelength of (b) UCDs and (c) HUCDs; (d) Fluorescence decay curve of UCDs and HUCDs.
TEM image of (a) UCDs and (b) HUCDs; Corresponding particle size distribution of (c) UCDs and (d) HUCDs; (e) FTIR spectra of UCDs and HUCDs.
XPS survey spectrum of (a) UCDs and (d) HUCDs; High-resolution C1s spectra of (b) UCDs and (e) HUCDs; High-resolution O1s spectra of (c) UCDs and (f) HUCDs.
Fluorescence intensity changes as the concentration of (a) UCDs and (b) HUCDs varied from 7.8 to 1000 μg/mL. Effects of pH values on fluorescence intensity of (c) UCDs and (d) HUCDs.
Bright field and fluorescence microscope images of Hela cells incubated with UCDs and HUCDs. Scale bar is 20 μm.

Bright field and fluorescence images of Caenorhabditis elegans treated with UCDs and HUCDs (20 mg/mL) after co-culture for 24 h. Scale bar is 500 μm. Bottom column diagram shows corresponding relative fluorescence intensity of the Caenorhabditis elegans treated with UCDs and HUCDs. Different letters of a, b, c represent significant difference (p < 0.05).
Confocal microscopy images of UCDs and HUCDs in onion epidermal cells excited by 405 nm wavelength. Scale bar is 100 μm. Different letters of a, b represent significant difference (p < 0.05).

Fluorescence images of bean sprouts treated with UCDs and HUCDs (20 mg/mL) for 24 h. (a) Photograph of bean sprouts under daylight, (b) Fluorescence images of bean sprouts soaked in deionized water and C-dot solution under UV light (365 nm), (c) Fluorescence images of bean sprouts after 24 h incubation under UV light (365 nm), (d) Fluorescence images of the internal bean sprouts stem after 24 h incubation under UV light (365 nm), (e) Column diagram shows corresponding relative fluorescence intensity of Fig. 8c, (f) Column diagram shows corresponding relative fluorescence intensity of Fig. 8d. Blank group was treated with deionized water. Different letters of a, b, c mean significant difference (p < 0.05).

The effect of (a) UCDs and (b) HUCDs on the number of NRK cells for 12 h; The ratio of dead NKR cells to living NKR cells after incubation with (c) UCDs and (d) HUCDs; (e) Hoechst 33,342 and PI double staining imaging of NRK cells after incubation with UCDs and HUCDs for 12 h. Scale bar is 1000 μm. Different letter of a, b, c represents significant difference (p < 0.05).
Schematic of the formation of C-dots from human urine and their application for bio-imaging.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots derived from urine and their application for bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University, 467 Zhongshan Road, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agronomy and Biotechnology, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to obtain water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) from low-value metabolites through a simple, economical, one-step synthetic route. The urine C-dots (UCDs) and hydrothermally treated urine C-dots (HUCDs) were obtained, respectively, using straightforward Sephadex filtration method from human adults and hydrothermal reaction method. The UCDs and HUCDs emit fluorescence upon being excited with ultraviolet light with a quantum yield of 4.8% and 17.8%, respectively. TEM analysis revealed that UCDs and HUCDs had an average size of 2.5 nm and 5.5 nm, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis showed the UCDs and HUCDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy demonstrated the presence of functional groups, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxylate and carbonyl groups onto the C-dots. The UCDs and HUCDs can be directly used for in vivo and in vitro imaging in Hela cells, Caenorhabditis elegans, onion epidermal cells and bean sprouts. The cytotoxicity study revealed that the UCDs and HUCDs were not toxic to normal rat kidney (NKR) cells with good biocompatibility. The results revealed that the C-dots derived from urine have good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence and may have potential to be a safe fluorescent probe for bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have received considerable attention as viable alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) for their unique properties like good biocompatibility, good photostability, high water-solubility, excitation tunable emission wavelength and flexibility in surface modification. Many studies have shown increasing potential in a broad range of applications, such as bioanalysis, biosensors [1–3], diagnostics [4] and bio-imaging [5–7]. To this end, many methods have been explored to prepare high-quality C-dots with high quantum yield and low cytotoxicity. Both natural and synthetic materials have been investigated for the fabrication of C-dots via different routes, such as oxidization carbon nanotube by-products [8], extraction of candle soot [9], hydrothermal cracking [10] and microwave synthesis of organic compounds [11,12]. The preparation of the C-dots has made pleasing progress through efforts of the past decade. However, the C-dots preparation still suffers from certain drawbacks such as tedious steps, involving the use of toxic reagents, special equipment, strong acid (alkali) and high temperature. Therefore, it is highly desired to explore the new precursors or resources for effective preparation C-dots in a low-cost manner.

Recently several studies have reported the existence of C-dots within nature products, like human food of breads [13], coffee [14]and roasted meat [15], which aroused our attention. Our idea is that after human consumption of these C-dots in foods, their metabolites such as urine may contain undigested C-dots. If these urine C-dots are isolated and used for biomedical applications, it would provide a green and low-cost route for the sustainable development of C-dots. The C-dots derived from human metabolites are safe and biology-friendly. However, the discovery or preparation of C-dots from animal or human metabolite source and exploration of their bio-imaging applications are still insufficiently studied.

In this paper, we reported the preparation of fluorescent C-dots from human urine (UCDs) by a facile chemistry separation method, and investigated the synthesis of hydrothermally-treated urine C-dots (HUCDs) using human urine as raw materials to improve fluorescence quantum yield (Scheme 1 ). Then the optical properties and morphology of the prepared UCDs and HUCDs were characterized, and the elemental composition of the prepared C-dots were examined to analyze their structure. The pH stability result showed that the UCDs and HUCDs might be used in a wide pH range for biological applications. Importantly, the cytotoxicity and bio-imaging evaluation of the UCDs and HUCDs revealed that the use of human metabolites as raw materials may provide a green and economical method for biocompatible C-dot preparation.

Fresh urine was collected from healthy human adults and used for C-dot preparation within one hour. Sephadex gel G-25 was bought from GE Healthcare (Fairfield, San Diego, USA). Acetic acid (CH3COOH, >99.5%) was purchased from Guanghua Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Quinine sulfate was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Inc. of China. Water used throughout all experiments was purified with the Millipore system. Cervical cancer HeLa cells were bought from ATCC (Manassas, VA, USA). Wild-type N2 Caenorhabditis elegans and Escherichia coli OP50 were provided by the Caenorhabditis Genetic Center (University of Minnesota, Minnesota, U.S.A.). All reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out with a JEOL model JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope using a 200 kV accelerating voltage to characterize the size and morphology of the C-dots. Absorption spectra of the C-dots were recorded with a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at ambient temperature. A Perkin Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded by an Escalab250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). To characterize the chemical composition. The functional groups were checked by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra on a VECTOR 22 instrument with the KBr pellet technique. Bio-imaging was performed by a FV1000 confocal fluorescent microscope (Olympus, Japan) with emission range of 425–525 nm and 500–600 nm for the excitation wavelength of 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively. Optical imaging of Caenorhabditis elegans was conducted with an inverted fluorescent microscope (Ti-S Nikon, Japan). Cytation™ 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader was used for real-time monitoring the growth of living cells in the presence of the C-dots. Lionheart TM automated live cell imager analyzer was used for live cell microscopy analysis and was optimized for live cell detection with emission range of 377–447 nm and 531–593 nm. Normal rat kidney (NKR) cell line was kindly donated by Li Yu lab of Tsinghua University, China.
Basically, the UCDs and HUCDs were prepared as follows:

To prepare the UCDs, 30 mL adult urine was condensed with rotating evaporator in vacuum, and then filtered with 0.22 μm membrane to remove the large particles. The filtrate was separated by Sephadex G-25 gel column with deionized water as eluent. After collection the fluorescent fractions, the solution was lyophilized to yield UCD powder, which was stored at −20 °C for further evaluation.

As for the synthesis of HUCDs, 30 mL filtered urine was added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heating at 200 °C for 8 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. Then the brown liquid was condensed with rotating evaporator and purified by Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatographic column using deionized water as eluent. The corresponding fluorescent fractions were collected and freeze dried at −50 °C. The white HUCDs powder was obtained and stored at −20 °C for further evaluation.

The fluorescence quantum yield of the C-dots was calculated by a reported method using the equation [7]:
(1)
Φ 1
=
Φ 2
I 1
A 2
η 1 2
/
I 2
A 1
η 2 2

In this equation, Φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield; I is the fluorescence intensity; A means the optical density; η means the refractive index; The subscripts 1 and 2 refer to the C-dots and the standard, respectively. The standard fluorescence quantum yield (Φ2) of quinine sulfate at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4 is 0.54 with a refractive index of 1.33. The refractive index of the C-dots dissolved in deionized water is also considered to be 1.33.

Fluorescence lifetime (τ) of UCDs and HUCDs were calculated using the following equation [5]:
(2)
τ =
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
+
A 3
τ 3
/
A 1
+
A 2
+
A 3
The average florescence lifetime of UCDs and HUCDs are 6.83 ns and 7.60 ns, respectively. Wherein Ai is the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on fluorescence intensity of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer contained 0.04 mol/L H3BO3, 0.04 mol/L H3PO4, and 0.04 mol/L CH3COOH was used to check the pH effect on fluorescence intensity of the C-dots. Various pH (2–11) BR solutions were prepared by adding different amount of 0.2 mol/L of NaOH solution. 200 μL of C-dots solution (1 mg·mL−1) was added to 2 mL of Britton–Robinson buffer solution and the fluorescence intensity was recorded with the PerkinElmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer using a 330 nm excitation wavelength and 1000 nm/min scan rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration effect on fluorescence intensity of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The UCDs and HUCDs were dissolved in deionized water to form different concentrations solution (7.8, 15.6, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000 μg·mL−1). The fluorescence intensity of C-dots solution was recorded with the PerkinElmer LS55 fluorescence spectrometer using a 330 nm excitation wavelength and 1000 nm/min scan rate. All measurements were performed in triplicate.

Cellular fluorescent images were recorded using an Olympus two-photon laser scanning confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan). Hela cells were cultivated in microscope dishes at a density of 1 × 105 per well in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. After removal of DMEM, 200 μL of the mixture of UCDs or HUCDs (1 mg·mL−1) in DMEM medium was added into each well and incubated for 4 h. Finally, the cells were thoroughly washed with PBS (phosphate buffer solution) buffer to remove extracellular C-dots and subjected for microscope analysis.

The C-dots were dissolved by the M9 buffer solution at a concentration of 20 mg·mL−1. The C-dots solution (100 μL) and the OP50 solution (20 μL) were added to 96-well plates, and then the Caenorhabditis elegans were seeded in the 96-well plates to incubate for 24 h. After 24 h incubation, the Caenorhabditis elegans were placed on a glass slide and observed by the Nikon Ti-S fluorescence microscope with excitation of 361–387 nm laser.

The fresh onion epidermis was incubated by the C-dots solution (3 mg·mL−1) for 6 h. After incubation, the onion epidermis was slowly washed with deionized water to remove the C-dots solution adhering to the surface. Then, the onion epidermis was placed on a glass slide and imaged with Confocal fluorescent microscopy with excitation of 405 nm laser.

The bean sprouts were seeded on 6-well-plates containing (20 mg·mL−1) C-dots and incubated for 24 h, deionized water as a blank. After 24 h incubation, the bean sprouts were slowly washed with deionized water and imaged with ZF-20D Dark box UV analyzer with excitation of 365 nm laser.

The NRK cells were seeded on 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well. The NRK cells were cultivated in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin streptomycin solution and 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, different concentrations (0, 0.031, 0.0625, 0.125, 0.25, 0.50 mg·mL−1) of UCDs or HUCDs in culture medium were added to 96-well plates, and the growth of NRK cells were dynamically monitored with Cytation™ 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader at 37 °C supplied with 5% CO2. After 12 h incubation, Hoechst 33,342 and propidium iodide (PI) were used to NRK cells double staining, and the number of living cells in the plates were counted.

In the past few years, a wide variety of materials have been explored for the synthesis of C-dots [1,5–7]. The C-dots are generally used as bio-imaging and bio-sensing probes due to their good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and high quantum yield [17]. However, research on the extraction of C-dots from human metabolites is rarely reported. The C-dots from human metabolites with good biocompatibility and convenient separation steps may provide an alternative strategy for a facile preparation of C-dots. The healthy human urine appears canary yellow, and emits blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). This motivated us to think that there might be C-dots within the fluorescent components based on our experience in coffee[5]. To this end, the UCDs were isolated form adult urine using a very simple and straightforward Sephadex filtration method. In order to further improve the fluorescence property of the C-dots, the urine was used as raw material for hydrothermal synthesis of the HUCDs.

To analyze the optical properties of UCDs and HUCDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured (Fig. 1 ). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of UCDs show two broad peaks at 275 and 325 nm, which indicating that the presence of the n − π* electronic transition of the C=O, C=C or N=N bonds [18]. After the hydrothermal treatment of the urine, the absorption peaks of HUCDs were significantly enhanced and blue-shifted to 232 and 290 nm, respectively. This strongly indicated the energy level was changed upon the hydrothermal treatment of the urine to form HUCDs, and the gap between the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) and the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) was increased. The HUCDs can absorb the shorter wavelength of light with higher energy to excite these electrons to higher anti-bonding molecular orbitals. The aqueous solutions of UCDs and HUCDs were completely transparent under daylight and strong fluorescence was observed when excited under UV light (365 nm) (Inset of Fig. 1a).

An excitation wavelength-dependent emission was observed (Fig. 1b and c), in which the emission intensity gradually increased and then decreased at 300–460 nm. The maximum emission of HUCDs shows a blue-shift at 380 nm as compared with that of UCDs at 400 nm. This is consistent with the result of UV–vis absorption blue-shift, indicating that the electron transitions from the relatively higher excited state to the ground state. The UCDs and HUCDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence, which is due to the complexity of the excited states of the C-dots, whose surface state may affect the band gap. This is an inherent characteristic of C-dots [19]. The fluorescence red-shift occurs in UCDs and HUCDs with increasing excitation wavelength, which is quite common in the fluorescent carbon materials and makes it possible to exhibit a variety of luminescent colors under different excitation light [20]. It has important significance in the application of fluorescence imaging. In comparison with quinine sulfate (reference), the measured quantum yields of UCDs and HUCDs were 4.8% and 17.8%, respectively. Obviously, the quantum yield of HUCDs has been significantly improved, which is higher than that of most C-dots within 10% [16]. High quantum yield of HUCDs may originate from the high nitrogen residues that lead to the trapping of more excitons under excitation [18]. A typical fluorescence lifetime (τ) was also measured (Fig. 1d), and the value for UCDs and HUCDs were 6.83 ns and 7.60 ns, respectively. It can be seen that the hydrothermal treated HUCDs have higher quantum yield and long fluorescence lifetime than the UCDs. It is useful for their biological imaging applications. Unlike the tedious preparation procedure of C-dots from urine sample via dehydration and heating for an additional 12 h at 200 °C to produce a black char [21], in this paper, we proved the presence and fluorescence characteristics of UCDs in human urine through a simple purification step, and found that the HUCDs with an improved quantum yield can be prepared after a facile one step hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the UCDs and HUCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a and 2b displays the representative TEM images of UCDs and HUCDs, revealing that these particles were spherical and well dispersed. The UCDs have size ranging from 1.0 to 5.0 nm (Fig. 2a), while HUCDs have size in the range of 2.0 to 9.0 nm (Fig. 2b). The average size of the UCDs and HUCDs are 2.5 and 5.5 nm, respectively, calculated based on 100 dots. The size of HUCDs was increased after the hydrothermal treatment, which is possibly due to the polymerization and conjugation during post-processing of urine.

The surface chemical groups of the prepared C-dots were further examined by FTIR. The UCDs clearly shows the peaks at 3220, 3350, 3450 cm−1 that are ascribed to the vibration of O–H and N–H (Fig. 2e). However, only a broad peak centering near 3500 cm−1 is observed for HUCDs because of the hydrothermal treatment effect, which might decrease the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. Meanwhile, a peak at 2950 cm−1 was also found indicating the presence of the C–H of the HUCDs. The peaks at 1600 and 1660 cm−1 corresponding to C=C/C=N from aromatic ring, and C=O vibrational stretch were decreased for HUCDs as compared with those of UCDs [18]. It is noteworthy that the peak at 1460 cm−1 for HUCDs almost disappeared, but the peak at 1390 cm−1 of nitryl was intensified. In addition, the peak at 1050 cm−1 (C-N vibrational stretch) and peak at 1140 cm−1 (C-O-C vibrational stretch) remained unchanged. All these FTIR results revealed the existence of carboxylate, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surfaces of the UCDs and HUCDs. After the hydrothermal reaction, the N–H, O–H and C=C groups were decreased, while the C-N, C-O-C and C=O groups presented distinct signal peak in HUCDs [20,22]. These results showed that there may be amidation reaction, C=C bond oxidation reaction and nitryl groups formation during the hydrothermal process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of the UCDs and HUCDs
ce:section.ce:para: What elements the C-dots contain is one of the concerns. The surface composition and chemical states of UCDs and HUCDs were characterized by XPS (Table 1 and Table 2 ). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XPS analysis shows two predominant peaks at 285, 532 eV and a weaker peak at 400 eV, which are attributed to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively. Small amount of S and Fe were also found in the C-dots. The elemental composition of UCDs was found to be C 67.8%, O 27.57%, and N 3.7%, which revealed their carbon-rich nature with the elements content in the order of C＞O＞N. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s could be fitted into four peaks at 283.4, 284.1, 284.9 and 286.2 eV (Fig. 3b), which can be ascribed to C–C bond, C=N or C=O, C=C, and C-O or C=N [23]. The high resolution spectra of O1s show major peaks at 531.4 eV and 531.9 eV for C=O and O-C=O groups, respectively (Fig. 3c) [24]. The elemental composition of HUCDs was similar to UCDs, and the content of C, O, and N in the HUCDs were 64.29%, 27.2% and 5.48%, respectively. High quantum yield of C-dots may due to the high nitrogen residues, which can capture more excitons under excitation [18]. The high resolution XPS spectra of C1s for HUCDs show the binding energy of C–C at 283.4 eV, C=C at 284.9 eV, and C-O or C=N at 286.2 eV (Fig. 3e), and O1s spectra represents the C=O and O-C=O groups with binding energy at 531.5 and 531.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 3f) [2,25]. It illustrates that these C-dots are actually belong to N-doped C-dots, which might come from the human metabolites like urea. Compared with the element composition of UCDs with HUCDs, the HUCDs has more nitrogen than UCDs. This is probably due to the reason that carbon and oxygen of urine were degraded as CO2 and H2O at high temperature during hydrothermal process. Combined with the results of FT-IR and XPS, the UCDs and HUCDs were functionalized with amino, hydroxyl, carboxylate and carbonyl groups, which are useful for increasing their water solubility and further surface conjugation

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of the UCDs and HUCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent property of C-dots solutions was subsequently investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensity was affected by different concentrations of UCDs and HUCDs and pH value. The fluorescence intensity of UCDs increased with the increasing concentration from 7.8 to 1000 μg/mL (Fig. 4a). In contrast, in the same concentration range, the fluorescence intensity of HUCDs gradually increased and then decreased (Fig. 4b). When the concentration was 62.5 μg/mL, the HUCDs showed the strongest fluorescence intensity with an obvious red shift when the concentration was gradually increased. The decrease of fluorescence intensity of HUCDs is possible due to the concentration quenching caused by the adjacent neighbor C-dots.

In addition, pH value exhibited a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 4c and 4d, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots showed a pH dependent behavior by varying the pH value from 2 to 11. Maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at basic pH environment. Pan et al. also found that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots gradually enhanced with pH value increasing from 1 to 13 [24]. This indicated when the pH value changed from acidic to alkaline, the radiative recombination of the energy-trapping sites on the C-dots was affected. It can be noted that the HUCDs are much brighter than the UCDs when the pH values around 7. Therefore, the HUCDs might have potential biological applications [26].

To assess the potential applications of the C-dots (UCDs and HUCDs) as fluorescent probes, Hela cells were used in the cellular uptake experiment. Fig. 5 displays that the Hela cells incubated with C-dots appear blue and green fluorescence when excited at the wavelengths of 405 and 488 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of HUCDs is stronger than that of UCDs, which is consistent with the results of their quantum yields. Both of the UCDs and HUCDs are distributed in cytoplasm of the cells. Although the particle size of HUCDs is larger than that of UCDs, it does not affect the C-dot distribution within cells. The translocation of C-dots from outside to the inside is probably through endocytosis [27]. The results indicated that the C-dots might be used as fluorescent probes for in vitro cell imaging.

Caenorhabditis elegans has a simple structure, short life cycle (3–4 weeks) and transparent body, which can be used as a good model organism in evaluation of C-dots fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 6 , the Caenorhabditis elegans treated with UCDs and HUCDs shows strong fluorescence compared with the blank group. The fluorescence was mainly located in the pharynx, intestine and anus of the Caenorhabditis elegans. This indicates that the UCDs and HUCDs are able to enter the body of Caenorhabditis elegans as they eat food OP50 (Escherichia coli). No significant biological toxicity of HUCDs and UCDs was observed for Caenorhabditis elegans when the concentrations of C-dots increased to 20 mg/mL. Under the same condition, stronger fluorescence intensity of HUCDs was found than that of the UCDs, indicating an improved fluorescence property after the hydrothermal treatment.

The UCDs and HUCDs were examined in onion epidermal cells imaging. As shown in Fig. 7 , blue fluorescence from C-dots was observed in the onion epidermal cells compared with the control, and the UCDs and HUCDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm of the onion epidermis. The UCDs and HUCDs entered onion epidermal cells, but mainly accumulated within the intracellular space, with no distribution in the cell wall. Similar result was also found in the kvass C-dots, which were purified from human beverages and showed clearly distribution in cytoplasm of onion epidermal cells [28]. The biodistribution of UCDs and HUCDs in onion epidermal cells provided more information about the ultra-small particle absorption in plant.

Bean sprouts were employed as a model plant to further investigate the in vivo distribution of C-dots (UCDs and HUCDs) in plant tissue. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the bean sprouts were soaked in 20 mg/mL C-dots for 24 h, respectively. Significant blue fluorescence of bean sprouts was found under the excitation of ultraviolet light (Fig. 8b). After 24 h incubation, the bean sprouts were taken out and imaged under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light (365 nm). The stems of the bean sprouts showed strong fluorescence, indicating that the C-dots entered the interior of the plant tissue and achieve fluorescent labeling (Fig. 8c). After separating the stems of the bean sprouts, the internal tissues also showed strong fluorescence (Figure d). The relative fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8 e and f) revealed that the HUCDs have stronger fluorescence intensity than the UCDs, which is consistent with the quantum yield analysis result of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity to normal cells of urine C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity is important property if the C-dots are used as fluorescence probes. The dynamic amounts of NRK cells incubated with UCDs and HUCDs were monitored by Cytation™ 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader for up to12 h. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, the growth of NRK cells treated with UCDs and HUCDs were promoted at the concentration of 0–0.5 mg/mL of C-dots. The growth of NRK cells was positively correlated with the concentration of C-dots. These results proved that the UCDs can promote the growth of NRK cells, which were consistent with the results by Essner et al. [21]. The living status of NRK cells was detected by Hoechst 33,342 and PI double staining. As shown in Fig. 9c and d, the ratio of dead cells to living cells was <1% for the UCDs, and 0.5% for HUCDs in the concentration of 0.0312 and 0.5 mg/mL. After Hoechst 33,342 and PI double staining, the cells show bright blue fluorescence and very little red fluorescence (Fig. 9e). Previous studies have shown that C-dots have little biological cytotoxicity [29]. Jiang et al. reported that C-dots extracted from commercial Nescafe instant coffee have no toxicity to cells at a concentration of 20 mg/mL [5]. Li et al. proved that C-dots from Coke and Pepsi did not cause obvious toxicity to cell viability at the concentration lower than 20 mg/mL [30]. Similarly, the urine C-dots showed good biosafety to the testing cells. The result showed that the UCDs have good compatibility, and the hydrothermally treated HUCDs did not cause significant cytotoxicity of the testing cells.

In summary, the UCDs were found and separated from human urine, and corresponding HUCDs were prepared using the human urine as raw materials. The absorption ability and fluorescence quantum yield of the HUCDs were improved. Both of the C-dots showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior because of the presence of multiple emission centers. The particle size increased from 2.5 nm (UCDs) to 5.5 nm (HUCDs) after the hydrothermal treatment due to the polymerization and conjugation. The XPS analysis indicated that they are N-doped C-dots, which mainly contain C, O, N and H from human metabolite of urea. The C-dots were successfully applied in live cells, Caenorhabditis elegans, onion epidermal cells and bean sprouts imaging. The cytotoxicity result demonstrated that the UCDs and HUCDs had good compatibility, and did not cause significant cytotoxicity of the testing cells. Therefore, the preparation of UCDs and HUCDs from human metabolites provide a green and economical method for their potential application in biological imaging.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ymeth.2019.04.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
